Website builder helping designers ship portfolios quickly.
Shipped public MVP and onboarded 130+ registered users.

## Overview

AUREA is a website builder shaped around one narrow use case: helping designers publish portfolios faster without getting trapped in heavy setup and repeated layout work.

## Problem

Many portfolio tools either prioritize generic templates or require too much manual configuration. Designers often need something between a blank canvas and a rigid site builder.

## Research

I looked at how portfolio creators struggle with the same tasks repeatedly:

- setting visual rhythm and spacing
- making work pages feel consistent
- handling responsive layout details
- shipping a public site without fighting the stack

That research pushed the product toward faster defaults and clearer structure.

## Architecture

The product was built as a frontend-heavy application with modular page composition, reusable layout patterns, and a backend layer for persistence and publishing workflows.

## Implementation

My work focused on frontend architecture, product direction, and making the editing experience feel lightweight. I worked on reusable interface patterns, content flow, and the path from draft to published portfolio.

## Challenges

The main challenge was balancing flexibility with clarity. Too much freedom turns the builder into work. Too little freedom makes every portfolio feel the same.

## Lessons Learned

Creative tools need opinionated structure. The best product decisions often came from removing options rather than adding them.

Interactive educational app for automata theory and algorithm exploration.
Built an interactive learning experience used for demo teaching sessions.

## Overview

Automata Visualizer is an educational tool built to make abstract theory easier to inspect through interaction rather than static explanation.

## Problem

Automata theory is often taught through diagrams and formal notation alone. That makes it harder for learners to build intuition around state changes and algorithm flow.

## Research

The work focused on how students understand transitions, inputs, and feedback when they can manipulate the system directly instead of only reading about it.

## Architecture

The application was structured around clear state representation, deterministic transitions, and interactive views that map directly to the underlying theory.

## Implementation

I built the educational interface and the logic needed to let users step through automata behavior visually.

## Challenges

The challenge was translating theory into interaction without oversimplifying the model or making the interface harder than the material itself.

## Lessons Learned

For educational software, a good explanation is often a good interaction pattern.

Public-facing website for a Telegram-based anti-scam bot built for Cambodian users.
Built a clear bilingual Astro site that explains the bot, its use cases, and how people should handle suspicious content.
The product needed to explain suspicious links, messages, and file scanning in plain language without overwhelming non-technical users.
Built the website in Astro with restrained styling, clear calls to action, and step-by-step sections for how the bot works.

## Overview

Broryat is the public website for a Telegram-based anti-scam bot. My role was focused on the site itself: shaping the information architecture, building the Astro frontend, and making the product understandable for everyday users.

## Problem

Security products often explain themselves in technical language. That creates a gap for people who most need the tool, especially when the real user question is simple: "Should I trust this message, link, or file?"

## Research

The content and structure were shaped around three needs:

- explain suspicious content in plain language
- make the product feel trustworthy without visual noise
- support both Khmer and English readers clearly

## Architecture

The site was built in Astro with a content-led structure, lightweight components, and static delivery. That kept performance strong and the editing model simple.

## Implementation

I built the frontend, structured the bilingual pages, and designed the section flow around quick comprehension. The emphasis was on clear hierarchy, concise copy, and immediate calls to action that lead users to the bot.

## Challenges

The hardest part was tone. The site needed to communicate urgency around online scams without becoming alarmist or visually chaotic.

## Lessons Learned

Trust on the web is often built through restraint. Clear language, predictable layout, and fast pages can do more than decorative security branding.

Automated external security scanner for modern web applications.
Built a scanner pipeline that inspects live surfaces without credentials, validates authentication and access patterns, and reports exploitable findings with remediation context.
Security issues in production apps are often exposed through public assets and configuration drift, but teams rarely have continuous external checks.

## Overview

LeakScope is an external security scanner built around a simple idea: many important security issues are already visible from the public internet if you know where to look and how to connect the signals.

## Problem

Security reviews are often periodic, manual, and limited by time. Public assets, client bundles, exposed configuration, weak access control flows, and token handling issues can stay live for long periods because nobody is checking them continuously.

## Research

I started by looking at recurring patterns in modern web applications:

- leaked secrets in bundled assets
- weak authorization around public endpoints
- JWT handling mistakes
- accidental exposure through configuration drift

The goal was not to simulate an internal pentest. It was to inspect only what an external attacker could already see.

## Architecture

The system is split into a few focused stages:

- collection of public assets and endpoints
- normalization and fingerprinting of findings
- rule-based analysis for auth and exposure issues
- report generation with remediation context

That separation made it easier to expand checks without turning the scanner into one large script.

## Implementation

I built the scanning pipeline, finding model, and reporting layer. The implementation focused on throughput, noise reduction, and clear evidence for each issue so the output stayed useful to engineering teams.

## Challenges

The hardest part was not finding signals. It was keeping the results trustworthy. External scanning can create noisy output quickly, so I spent a lot of time tuning detection logic, validating edge cases, and reducing false positives.

## Lessons Learned

Security tools only help when the output is readable and actionable. Good detection matters, but clear prioritization and remediation context matter just as much.

Creative freelancing platform connecting local digital artists with clients.
Delivered production-ready frontend architecture for marketplace flows.

## Overview

Phsar Design is a creative marketplace focused on helping local designers present their work and connect with clients in a more structured way.

## Problem

Independent creatives often rely on scattered social platforms that are good for visibility but weak for discovery, trust, and service presentation.

## Research

The product direction focused on how creatives present themselves, how clients compare work, and where marketplace flows usually break:

- unclear profiles
- weak service framing
- inconsistent browsing experience
- too much friction before first contact

## Architecture

The frontend was designed around reusable marketplace views, predictable state management, and a simple browsing flow that could scale with more content and categories.

## Implementation

I worked on frontend architecture and user-facing flows, including listing structure, navigation, and the interface patterns needed for a marketplace experience.

## Challenges

Marketplace products become messy quickly if the hierarchy is weak. A lot of the work was about keeping browsing and discovery simple while the product surface grew.

## Lessons Learned

Creative platforms work best when the interface gets out of the way and lets the work speak clearly.

Offline-first stock management mobile app with practical analytics.
Implemented reliable CRUD, stock movement tracking, and low-stock alerts.

## Overview

StockMate is a mobile stock management app built for straightforward day-to-day inventory tracking, especially in environments where internet access is not always reliable.

## Problem

Small operations still need dependable stock records, but lightweight inventory tools often trade reliability for convenience or assume constant connectivity.

## Research

The focus was on the basic jobs users repeat every day:

- create and update stock items
- record movement clearly
- catch low-stock situations early
- keep the app usable offline

## Architecture

The app used an offline-first structure with local persistence, clear domain boundaries, and straightforward data flows for inventory operations.

## Implementation

I handled the mobile implementation, CRUD flows, stock movement logic, and low-stock alert behavior.

## Challenges

Offline-first apps need careful handling of state and persistence. The work was less about visual complexity and more about reliability.

## Lessons Learned

In operational tools, dependable basics matter more than feature volume.

What public-facing systems reveal before login
External security work often starts with small signals that teams stop seeing because they are too close to the system.

One of the most useful shifts in security thinking is realizing how much can already be learned from the outside.

Before credentials, before internal docs, and before source access, a public application still exposes a lot:

- client bundles
- routing behavior
- token handling patterns
- environment drift
- infrastructure fingerprints

None of these signals guarantee a vulnerability on their own. The value comes from how they connect.

This is why external scanning is interesting to me. It forces discipline. You do not get to assume context. You have to reason from evidence, sequence, and behavior.

The practical lesson is simple: teams should review their products the way an outsider sees them. If sensitive assumptions are visible from the browser, they are already part of the attack surface.

Security work gets better when it stays concrete. Start with what the system is already saying in public.

Quiet interfaces are usually the hardest ones
The calmer a product feels, the more careful the underlying decisions usually are.

Good interfaces rarely feel busy by accident. When a page feels calm, it usually means somebody spent time removing friction, aligning hierarchy, and deciding what does not belong there.

I keep coming back to the same pattern in product work: if everything asks for attention, nothing actually receives it. Noise can look like effort, but it usually weakens confidence.

The hardest part is not adding visual treatment. The hard part is choosing the one thing the user needs to notice first and shaping the rest around that decision.

For me, that usually means:

- using typography to create rhythm before adding decoration
- making spacing do more structural work
- reducing interface choices until the main path becomes obvious
- keeping interaction feedback subtle and predictable

This is one reason I like working on products that need trust. Calm interfaces are easier to read, easier to navigate, and easier to believe.

Clarity is not the absence of design. It is design with enough discipline to stop early.

Teaching JavaScript changed how I write it
Explaining code to beginners forces a level of clarity that improves everyday engineering work too.

Teaching JavaScript to newer developers made one thing obvious very quickly: code that is hard to explain is often harder than it needs to be.

When students struggle, it is not always because the topic is advanced. Sometimes the real problem is that the code path contains too many hidden assumptions.

Teaching pushed me to pay more attention to:

- naming that reduces guesswork
- functions with one obvious job
- data flow that can be described in a sentence
- examples that expose edge cases early

This carries back into product work. Clear code is easier to review, easier to change, and easier to trust under pressure.

I still like elegant solutions, but I trust explainable ones more. If a teammate or student can follow the reasoning without ceremony, the implementation usually has a stronger foundation.
